OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 60-year-old male hospitalized with COVID-19 for respiratory status, oxygen needs, exertional tolerance, and complications consistent with moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS).

Patient_Actor.History: Hospitalized due to COVID-19 with fever, dry cough, and dyspnea. During physical therapy on the acute ward, any change of position or deep breathing triggered coughing attacks that caused oxygen desaturation and worsening dyspnea. Position changes had to be performed very slowly and step-by-step to avoid rapid deterioration and respiratory failure. A 135° prone position change took around 30 minutes and was tolerated. Breathing exercises had to be modified to avoid prolonged coughing and desaturation. The patient also had difficulty maintaining sufficient oxygen saturation during physical activity, but with close monitoring and frequent breaks he was able to perform low-level strength and walking exercises without significant deoxygenation. Exercise progression was low on days 1 to 5 and then increased daily until discharge to a rehabilitation clinic on day 10.

Secondary_Symptoms: Coughing attacks triggered by position changes or deep breathing
Oxygen desaturation with coughing or exertion

Vital_Signs.Oxygen_Saturation: 93% on day 5 while receiving 6 L/min oxygen, improved to 97% after slow position change

Respiratory_Examination.Work_of_Breathing: Dyspnea noted, especially with coughing attacks and exertion
Respiratory_Examination.Cough: Dry cough; coughing attacks triggered by deep breathing and position changes

Functional_Status.Position_Change_Tolerance: Required very slow, step-by-step position changes; 135° prone positioning took around 30 minutes
Functional_Status.Exercise_Tolerance: Low-level strength and walking exercises tolerated with close monitoring and frequent breaks

Oxygenation.Result: Oxygen desaturation occurred with coughing, deep breathing, and physical activity
Oxygenation.Example: On day 5, oxygen saturation increased from 93% to 97% on 6 L/min oxygen after slow prone positioning

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) due to COVID-19

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case text states the patient was "hospitalized due to moderate ARDS from COVID-19."

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 66-year-old male with fever, dyspnea, cough, asthenia, poor appetite, nausea, and vomiting, and determine the cause of worsening oxygen requirements and prolonged ventilator dependence in the setting of COVID-19.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient initially developed fever, dyspnea, coughing, asthenia, lack of appetite, nausea, and vomiting. He was admitted to an acute care unit for observation and oxygen therapy, but his oxygen requirements kept increasing. He later had prolonged deep sedation, neuromuscular blocking agents, and proning, followed by difficulty weaning from mechanical ventilation. Tracheostomy was performed because of persistent difficult weaning, probably related to respiratory muscle weakness. He later improved physically but still had fatigue, inspiratory muscle weakness, and dysphagia at transfer to a step-down unit.

Vital_Signs.Oxygen_Requirement: Increasing oxygen requirements; later required mechanical ventilation

Respiratory_Examination.Ventilator_Status: Prolonged ventilator-dependent respiratory failure; tracheostomy performed

Functional_Status.Mobility: Improved to active movements, chair transfer with assistance, active in-bed cycling against resistance, and walking distance of 10 m

Imaging_and_Respiratory_Assessment.Diagnosis_Context: Moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) in the setting of COVID-19

Rehabilitation: Chair transfer with help of 2 physical therapists
Active in-bed cycling against resistance for 20 minutes
Increase in pressure support by 5 cmH2O during efforts

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 with moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and prolonged ventilator-dependent respiratory failure

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case states the patient developed fever, dyspnea, coughing, and worsening oxygen needs "due to moderate ARDS," required deep sedation, neuromuscular blockade, proning, and later tracheostomy for difficult weaning.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 77-year-old male with known COVID-19 who has worsening respiratory status and determine the need for escalation of care, including ICU-level management and airway support.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient was diagnosed with COVID-19 and then transferred to the ICU 1 week later because of continuing respiratory decompensation requiring intubation. After the acute phase, he required intermittent proning and remained hemodynamically unstable and difficult to wean from ventilatory support. Two weeks later, he underwent tracheostomy and began to improve very slowly.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Worsening respiratory decompensation after COVID-19 diagnosis
Secondary_Symptoms: Severe weakness after prolonged critical illness
Limited ability to speak initially after tracheostomy

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: COVID-19 infection with progression to critical illness. Other past medical history is not specified in source.

General_Examination.Appearance: Critically ill patient requiring ICU care; exact bedside appearance not specified in source.
General_Examination.Respiratory_Status: Required intubation and later tracheostomy; detailed lung examination findings are not specified in source.
General_Examination.Hemodynamic_Status: Hemodynamically unstable during the ICU course.

COVID_19_Testing.Result: Positive COVID-19 diagnosis prior to ICU transfer

Respiratory_Support.Proning: Intermittent proning used during acute phase
Respiratory_Support.Tracheostomy: Performed 2 weeks after ICU course began

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 with acute respiratory failure requiring ICU care, intubation, and tracheostomy

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case states the patient was transferred to the ICU after his COVID-19 diagnosis due to continuing respiratory decompensation requiring intubation, later tracheotomized, indicating severe COVID-19 respiratory failure.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a primary care patient with persistent fever, dry cough, worsening shortness of breath, and reduced general health to identify the cause of acute respiratory illness and determine need for hospital-level care.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient reports persistent fever and dry cough for 2 weeks with progressively worsening general health. He became increasingly short of breath, especially with minor physical activity, and was hospitalized because of his worsening condition. He initially required oxygen at 4 L/min. He describes rapid, shallow breathing at rest and severe breathlessness with minimal exertion. He also reports significant anxiety related to fear of dying and concern about his future, which seemed to worsen his breathlessness.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Persistent fever and dry cough with worsening shortness of breath
Secondary_Symptoms: Severe breathlessness during minor physical activities

Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: Not specified in source beyond fever, dry cough, dyspnea, anxiety, and weakness.

Vital_Signs.Respiratory_Rate: 30 breaths/min initially; reduced to 22 breaths/min after deep-breathing exercises on day 2
Vital_Signs.Oxygen_Saturation: 92% on 4 L/min oxygen initially; increased to 96% on 4 L/min oxygen after deep-breathing exercises on day 2

Respiratory_Examination.Breathing_Pattern: Rapid and shallow breathing at rest
Respiratory_Examination.Dyspnea: Severe breathlessness with minor physical activity

Functional_Status.Mobility: Barely able to walk to the toilet initially
Functional_Status.Exercise_Tolerance: Improved over the hospital course; later able to walk 350 m without a walking aid or supplemental oxygen before discharge

Oxygen_Requirement.Course: Eventually no supplemental oxygen required before discharge

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 with acute hypoxemic respiratory illness requiring hospitalization

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 57-year-old man with worsening dyspnea and oxygen desaturation after a recent positive COVID-19 test, determine the severity of illness, and initiate appropriate primary care/acute referral management.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient reports that about one week after a positive COVID-19 test, he developed worsening shortness of breath with marked breathing difficulty. He became progressively more tachypneic and dyspneic, with oxygen desaturation reported to 70% prior to ICU admission. He is highly short of breath and becomes significantly worse with even slight effort.

Secondary_Symptoms: Marked symptom exacerbation with minimal exertion

Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: Not specified in source beyond respiratory symptoms.

Vital_Signs.Oxygen_Saturation: Reported desaturation to 70% before ICU admission; 88% improved to 96% with prone positioning and deep-breathing exercises

General_Examination.Work_of_Breathing: Increased, with significant symptom exacerbation from the slightest effort

Respiratory_Examination.Findings: Profoundly reduced respiratory capacity is described; other specific lung exam findings are not specified in source

COVID_19_Test.Timing: Approximately one week before ICU admission

Oxygenation_Assessment.During_Physical_Therapy: 88% increased to 96% after prone positioning and deep-breathing exercises

Respiratory_Support.Oxygen_Therapy: 6 to 12 L/min over the next days

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Severe COVID-19 pneumonia with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case states the patient had a positive COVID-19 result, was admitted to the ICU for oxygen desaturation (70%) with worsening tachypnea and dyspnea, required 6 to 12 L/min oxygen therapy, and intubation was avoided, indicating severe COVID-19 respiratory illness with hypoxemia.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 69-year-old man with suspected severe COVID-19 pneumonia and respiratory failure, recognize deterioration to acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and manage as a critically ill primary care referral/emergency presentation.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient had a dry cough for 2 weeks before admission. He was admitted to the ICU because oxygenation was poor and computed tomographic imaging showed typical COVID-19 pneumonia. He initially received lung-protective ventilation and targeted sedation. Over the next days, he deteriorated with hemodynamic instability and severe ARDS, requiring intermittent prone positioning and continuous renal replacement therapy. After tracheostomy, passive range-of-motion exercises and passive side-edge mobilization were slowly resumed, but asynchronous ventilation and hemodynamic instability remained major problems, leading to further sedation and relaxation. The patient later died after withdrawal of life support.

Secondary_Symptoms: Typical COVID-19 pneumonia on CT imaging

Physical_Examination_Findings.General_Examination: Critically ill ICU patient with severe respiratory compromise; further sedation and relaxation were required due to asynchronous ventilation and hemodynamic instability.
Physical_Examination_Findings.Respiratory_Examination: Poor oxygenation was present. Specific auscultatory findings are not specified in source.

Other_Examination_Findings.Positioning_and_Mobility: Required lung-protective ventilation, targeted sedation, intermittent prone positioning, tracheostomy, passive range-of-motion exercises, and passive side-edge mobilization.
Other_Examination_Findings.Skin: A small pressure ulcer on the forehead developed after prolonged and repeated positioning.
Other_Examination_Findings.Functional_Status: After 24 days in the ICU, the patient scored 1/50 on the Chelsea Critical Care Physical Assessment Tool (CPAx) and showed severe signs of muscle loss.

Respiratory_Support.Ventilation: Lung-protective ventilation initially; later asynchronous ventilation noted.

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Severe COVID-19 pneumonia complicated by acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case states that CT imaging showed typical COVID-19 pneumonia and that the patient later developed "severe ARDS" with hemodynamic instability; the title and clinical course identify this as a COVID-19 acute care case.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 57-year-old male with confirmed COVID-19 who presents with dyspnea, heavy dry cough, and fever, and determine the severity of respiratory compromise, including need for ICU-level care and ventilatory support.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient was admitted to the ICU 6 days after testing positive for COVID-19. He initially had dyspnea, a heavy dry cough, and fever. His respiratory status progressively worsened despite initial ability to exercise and sit in a chair with a physical therapist, eventually requiring intubation and proning. After extubation, he remained unable to effectively clear mucus because of a weak cough and continued to require frequent airway clearance interventions.

Respiratory_Examination.Inspection: Respiratory distress implied by dyspnea and progressive respiratory failure; intubation was required.
Respiratory_Examination.Auscultation: Crackles were present on pulmonary auscultation.
Respiratory_Examination.Airway_Secretions: Large amounts of bronchial mucus requiring regular suctioning.
Respiratory_Examination.Ventilator_Assessment: Expiratory flow showed a sawtooth pattern.

Respiratory_Support.Intubation: Required due to progressive respiratory failure

Airway_Clearance_Assessment.Suctioning_Response: Suctioning did not adequately improve clinical signs, prompting manual airway clearance techniques

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring ICU care and mechanical ventilation

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case states the patient was admitted after testing positive for COVID-19 and had progressive respiratory failure necessitating intubation and proning, with ongoing ICU care without ventilatory support at the time of writing.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 52-year-old man with recent COVID-19 infection, pneumonia progressing to moderate ARDS, and post-extubation swallowing and communication difficulties. Identify the likely diagnosis, evaluate respiratory recovery, neurologic/functional status, and aspiration risk, and determine the need for dysphagia management and rehabilitation referral.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient tested positive for COVID-19 after 4 days of dry cough, fever, and head and limb pain. One day later, he was hospitalized because of exertional dyspnea. He was diagnosed with pneumonia that progressed to moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), requiring mechanical ventilation and intermittent dialysis. After extubation, his oxygenation remained stable on 2 to 3 L/min of oxygen. However, he was disoriented and could not communicate verbally. He had marked global weakness, oral and pharyngeal weakness, paresthesia, reduced spontaneous swallowing frequency, reduced tongue control, and poor protection against aspiration. He was treated nil by mouth and received dysphagia therapy. Over time, he improved to single-word phrases, then to swallowing puréed food and mildly thick fluids under supervision, and eventually to independent food ingestion before discharge to rehabilitation.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Post-COVID respiratory illness with severe swallowing difficulty after critical illness

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: COVID-19 pneumonia complicated by moderate ARDS requiring mechanical ventilation and intermittent dialysis during hospitalization.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: After extubation, oxygenation was stable on 2 to 3 L/min of oxygen. No additional review of systems details are specified in the source.

Vital_Signs.Oxygen_Requirement: Stable on 2 to 3 L/min of oxygen after extubation

General_Examination.Communication: Initially unable to communicate verbally; later able to communicate in single-word phrases
General_Examination.Functional_Status: Markedly reduced early, improving over time

Swallowing_Assessment.Oral_Pharyngeal_Function: Oral and pharyngeal weakness with paresthesia
Swallowing_Assessment.Oral_Secretions: Massive oral and pharyngeal dry saliva residuals during the severe phase

COVID_19_Test.Timing: 4 days after onset of dry cough, fever, and head and limb pain

Gugging_Swallowing_Screen.Interpretation: Able to swallow puréed food and mildly thick fluids under supervision, but cough strength still weak

Diet_Progression: Puréed food and mildly thick fluids under supervision

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 pneumonia complicated by moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and severe dysphagia after critical illness

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case explicitly states the patient tested COVID-19 positive, was diagnosed with pneumonia that developed into moderate ARDS needing mechanical ventilation and intermittent dialysis, and later had severe dysphagia confirmed by the Gugging Swallowing Screen.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 59-year-old female with severe dyspnea and hypoxemia, recognize COVID-19 pneumonia complicated by moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and identify subsequent ICU-acquired weakness during recovery.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient was found by paramedics with dyspnea and severe hypoxemia. She required immediate tracheal intubation. During the ICU course, she was treated for moderate ARDS with deep sedation, neuromuscular blocking agents, and prone positioning. As she improved, she underwent progressive physical rehabilitation, including sitting on the edge of the bed, transfer to a chair, and later walking with a walking aid. She had postextubation dysphagia and ongoing weakness consistent with ICU-acquired weakness.

Secondary_Symptoms: Reduced lung compliance during ICU course

Respiratory_Examination.Findings: Severe respiratory distress at presentation requiring immediate tracheal intubation. Moderate ARDS with reduced lung compliance was diagnosed during ICU care.

Neuromuscular_Function.Findings: Muscle strength indicated ICU-acquired weakness with Medical Research Council sum-score (MRC-SS) of 40/60 during recovery.
Neuromuscular_Function.Additional_Notes: Later improved but persistent physical function impairment remained.

Functional_Status.Findings: Initially required 3 physical therapists to maintain sitting on the edge of the bed; later could hold her head and sit for about 15 minutes with 2 therapists; later walked with a walking aid.

Arterial_Oxygenation.Result: 65% oxygenation on room air at presentation

Respiratory_Diagnosis.Result: Moderate ARDS with reduced lung compliance

Exercise_Oxygenation.Result: 89% with 3 L/min oxygen during exercise training on day 19

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 pneumonia complicated by moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and ICU-acquired weakness

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case states she was found with severe dyspnea and hypoxemia, then "Moderate ARDS with reduced lung compliance was diagnosed" and treated with intubation, sedation, neuromuscular blockade, and prone positioning; the report is a COVID-19 case report series.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 33-year-old woman with recent severe COVID-19 illness requiring ICU care, intubation, and extubation, and identify complications affecting breathing, swallowing, strength, and mental status. Focus on current functional status, respiratory recovery, swallowing safety, weakness, and delirium/anxiety.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient had typical COVID-19 symptoms about 1 week before ICU admission, including high fever, dry cough, headache, and dyspnea. Her respiratory status rapidly deteriorated, leading to intubation and proning. After several days in the ICU, she improved and was extubated. Following extubation, she developed difficulty swallowing, severe weakness, and marked confusion/anxiety. She repeatedly stated that she had been abducted and believed she had to die. She appeared frightened and was difficult to calm. Her husband was allowed to visit because her anxiety was limiting rehabilitation, which briefly helped her feel safer and participate in basic activities.

Primary_Symptoms: Delirium with anxiety and paranoid statements

Review_of_Systems.Respiratory: Recent dyspnea prior to ICU admission; current respiratory status after extubation is not fully specified in source.
Review_of_Systems.Neurologic_Psychiatric: Confusion, delirium, anxiety, paranoid beliefs, and fearfulness.

General_Examination.Appearance: Anxious, frightened, and difficult to calm.
General_Examination.Mental_Status: Delirious with paranoid statements; believed she had been abducted and that she had to die.

Functional_Status.Rehabilitation_Status: Able to participate in some basic functional activities after reassurance; CPAx 21/50.

ICU_Course.Respiratory_Support: Intubated and proned due to rapid respiratory deterioration.
ICU_Course.Duration_of_Instability: For the following 6 days, her situation was unstable.
ICU_Course.Improvement: From day 7 onwards, she started to improve rapidly and could be mobilized passively into a side-edge position.

Disposition.Transfer_Status: Delirium did not resolve upon transfer to a peripheral acute hospital.

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 with acute respiratory failure requiring ICU care, complicated by postextubation dysphagia, ICU-acquired weakness, and delirium

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case states she had typical COVID-19 symptoms before ICU admission, was intubated and proned due to rapid respiratory deterioration, and later had postextubation dysphagia, severe ICU-acquired weakness, and pronounced delirium.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 66-year-old man with acute respiratory deterioration and neurologic deficits, identify the likely diagnosis, and determine the severity and immediate management needs in a primary care context.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient was admitted to the hospital with an ischemic left-hemispheric stroke along with a dry cough and fever. He tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 the following day and then deteriorated, developing severe acute respiratory distress syndrome requiring intubation and ICU admission. He later required veno-venous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation for 7 days. After sedation was stopped, he remained somnolent, unable to communicate, and unable to follow commands. He also had marked weakness, especially on the hemiplegic side, and reduced self-activity. Therapy notes describe suspected impaired perception and visual acuity, reduced ability to initiate movement, and persistent functional dependence.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Dry cough and fever with progressive respiratory failure
Secondary_Symptoms: Marked weakness, especially on the hemiplegic side
Suspected impaired perception and visual acuity

General_Examination.Mental_Status: Somnolent after sedation was stopped; unable to communicate or follow commands
General_Examination.Mobility: Severely reduced mobility with poor trunk and head control during therapy

Neurologic_Examination.Motor: Severely reduced muscle tone and strength, particularly on the hemiplegic side

Respiratory_Support.Proning: Repeatedly performed with insufficient improvement in gas exchange

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: COVID-19 pneumonia complicated by severe acute respiratory distress syndrome, with concurrent ischemic left hemispheric stroke

title: Early Physical Therapist Interventions for Patients With COVID-19 in the Acute Care Hospital: A Case Report Series
diagnosis_evidence: The case explicitly states the patient was admitted due to an ischemic left-hemispheric stroke and then tested positive for SARS-CoV-2, deteriorating to severe ARDS requiring intubation, proning, and veno-venous ECMO.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a malnourished primary care patient with new right upper quadrant pain and abnormal liver tests, identify the likely biliary cause, and determine the need for urgent referral and initial management.

History.Presenting_Concern: Recently developed right upper quadrant abdominal pain during an inpatient admission after collapsing at home from severe hypoglycemia.
History.Symptom_Description: Right upper quadrant pain was mentioned on the following day after an episode of decreased consciousness and respiratory depression. The source does not specify the exact character, severity, radiation, or triggers of the pain.
Associated_Symptoms: Memory problems from an old traumatic brain injury
History.Relevant_Context: She was admitted after a hypoglycemic collapse and had a prolonged inpatient course complicated by infection, opioid-related respiratory depression reversed with naloxone, and worsening liver enzymes before biliary imaging was obtained.

Past_Medical_History: Old traumatic brain injury with memory problems
Multiple prior admissions to eating disorder centres for nasogastric feeding
Social_History: Well known to mental health services

Abdominal_Examination.Findings: Not specified in source beyond later right upper quadrant pain

Other_Examination.Findings: The rest of the clinical examination was normal

Imaging.Chest_Radiograph: Patchy consolidations in the right middle and lower lobes
Imaging.Abdominal_Ultrasound: Multiple small calculi and biliary debris, with gallbladder oedema and a small amount of peri-cholecystic fluid

Laboratory_Trends.Liver_Enzymes: Worsening liver enzymes were noted after onset of right upper quadrant pain; specific values are not provided in the source
Laboratory_Trends.Inflammatory_Markers: Improved after treatment with amoxicillin, clavulanic acid, and clarithromycin; specific values are not provided in the source

Other_Clinical_Events.Respiratory_Depression_Event: Decreased consciousness, bradypnoea (RR 6), and hypotension (83/64) improved after naloxone
Other_Clinical_Events.Capacity: She was deemed to lack capacity to refuse treatment and Section 5(2) under the Mental Health Act was put in place

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Acute cholecystitis with gallstones (calculous cholecystitis) in the setting of severe malnutrition/anorexia nervosa

title: Deranged Liver Function Tests and Liver Insults in Malnourished Patients: A Report of Two Cases and Literature Review
diagnosis_evidence: The case states that abdominal ultrasound showed multiple small calculi, biliary debris, gallbladder oedema, and peri-cholecystic fluid, and that "She was treated for cholecystitis" with antibiotics, with subsequent improvement. MRCP was negative for intra-biliary pathology.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 29-year-old man who collapsed at home, identify the cause of hypoglycaemia and severe malnutrition, evaluate for an eating disorder, and recognize associated deranged liver function tests.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient was brought to hospital by ambulance after collapsing at home. He has a history of eating and anxiety disorders and is not taking any regular medications. He may appear to have limited insight into his condition.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Collapse at home with documented hypoglycaemia
Secondary_Symptoms: Severe weight loss / very low body weight

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: History of eating disorder and anxiety disorder.

System_Examination.Findings: The rest of the clinical examination was normal.

Liver_Function_Tests.Details: Specific values not provided in source

Liver_Synthetic_Function.Result: Improved over the course of admission
Liver_Synthetic_Function.Details: Specific values not provided in source

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Severe malnutrition due to eating disorder (anorexia nervosa) with hypoglycemia and deranged liver function tests

title: Deranged Liver Function Tests and Liver Insults in Malnourished Patients: A Report of Two Cases and Literature Review
diagnosis_evidence: The case describes a 29-year-old man with a history of eating and anxiety disorders, profound underweight/cachexia (BMI 11.6), hypoglycemia, and markedly abnormal liver function tests that improved with nutritional rehabilitation and transfer to an Eating Disorders Unit, indicating malnutrition related to an eating disorder as the final diagnosis.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 24-year-old woman with anorexia nervosa who presents with severe malnutrition, dehydration, jaundice, and worsening liver injury; identify the most likely cause of the acute liver injury and exclude other common etiologies.

History.Presenting_Problem: Admitted for severe malnutrition and dehydration in the context of anorexia nervosa diagnosed one year ago.
History.Course_of_Illness: She had been hospitalized for 4 months with a transitory gain of six kilograms. On admission, she was lethargic and slightly jaundiced. Despite stopping treatment and undergoing careful progressive refeeding, her laboratory tests continued to deteriorate initially.
History.Eating_Disorder_History: Anorexia nervosa diagnosed one year ago.
History.Medications: Treated with a serotonin reuptake inhibitor.
History.Substance_Use: Denied taking illicit drugs or potentially harmful products.
History.Associated_Symptoms: Not specified in source beyond lethargy and slight jaundice.
History.Abdominal_Symptoms: The abdomen was slightly tender without ascites.

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Anorexia nervosa diagnosed one year ago.
Patient_Actor.Social_History: Denied illicit drug use or use of potentially harmful products.

General_Examination.Appearance: Oriented but lethargic and slightly jaundiced.

Histology.Liver_Biopsy: Architecture of the liver was normal without fibrosis. Portal tracts and biliary and vascular structures were normal. Cytoplasm of hepatocytes was clarified and some acidophil bodies were found, predominantly in the centrilobular region. Ceroid pigments at the apical pole of hepatocytes were observed.

Electron_Microscopy.Findings: Features strongly suggested autophagocytosis: very low glycogen density in hepatocytes, reduced mitochondria and rough endoplasmic reticulum, increased Golgi bodies and smooth endoplasmic reticulum, and primary and secondary lysosomes containing digested material.

Outcome.Eight_Weeks_After_Admission: With progressive refeeding, the tests returned back to normal.

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Acute liver injury due to starvation-induced hepatocyte autophagy in anorexia nervosa

title: Severe Starvation-Induced Hepatocyte Autophagy as a Cause of Acute Liver Injury in Anorexia Nervosa: A Case Report
diagnosis_evidence: The title explicitly states "Severe Starvation-Induced Hepatocyte Autophagy as a Cause of Acute Liver Injury in Anorexia Nervosa," and the case text concludes that "a diagnosis of acute liver injury associated with anorexia nervosa was considered the most plausible diagnosis" with biopsy and electron microscopy strongly suggesting autophagocytosis.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 36-year-old premenopausal woman with hormone receptor-positive multifocal right breast invasive mammary carcinoma who has developed return of regular menses while receiving goserelin ovarian suppression, and determine whether ovarian suppression has failed.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient has a history of multifocal right breast invasive mammary carcinoma with ductal and lobular features. She previously underwent right breast simple mastectomy with axillary lymph node evaluation, adjuvant post-mastectomy radiation therapy, and was started on ovarian suppression with goserelin 3.6 mg every 28 days plus letrozole 2.5 mg daily. Sixteen months after starting goserelin, she noticed that her menstrual cycles returned and became regular despite continuing the injections. She reports no known missed doses and no medication interaction was identified. She is concerned about what this means for her breast cancer treatment and is hesitant about surgical menopause/oophorectomy.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Return of regular menstrual cycles while on goserelin
Secondary_Symptoms: Concern about adequacy of ovarian suppression

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Hormone receptor-positive multifocal right breast invasive mammary carcinoma; status post right breast simple mastectomy and axillary lymph node evaluation; adjuvant PMRT; genetic testing without clinically significant mutations.

Breast_Imaging.Diagnostic_Mammogram_and_Ultrasound: Hypoechoic lesion in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast measuring 14 mm x 13 mm x 18 mm, with a 5 x 4 mm satellite lesion 6 mm inferior to the dominant mass; BI-RADS 5, highly suggestive of malignancy.

Breast_MRI.Findings: 2.3 x 1.1 x 2.7 cm irregular-shaped heterogeneous mass with irregular margins in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast, 7 cm from the nipple and 1.2 cm from the skin; additional mass measuring 8 mm x 4 mm x 1.6 cm at 12:00; additional 4 mm lesion 7 mm from the nipple at 10:00.

Biopsy.Diagnosis: Invasive mammary carcinoma with features of both lobular and ductal carcinoma
Biopsy.Receptor_Status: Estrogen receptor 90%, progesterone receptor 100%, HER2 2+ by IHC but negative by FISH, Ki-67 50%

Surgical_Pathology.Diagnosis: Multifocal invasive mammary carcinoma of the breast with ductal and lobular features
Surgical_Pathology.DCIS: Low to intermediate nuclear grade DCIS without central necrosis measuring at least 6 mm

Oncotype_DX.Comment: For patients <50 years of age, benefit from chemotherapy 1.6%

Ovarian_Suppression_Failure_Assessment.Menstrual_Status: Regular menstrual cycle resumed while on goserelin
Ovarian_Suppression_Failure_Assessment.Interpretation: Premenopausal range, consistent with failure of ovarian suppression/ablation

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Failure of ovarian suppression/ablation with goserelin in a premenopausal woman with hormone receptor-positive multifocal right breast invasive mammary carcinoma

title: Goserelin Ovarian Ablation Failure in Premenopausal Women With Breast Cancer
diagnosis_evidence: The case explicitly states that 16 months after starting goserelin ovarian suppression, the patient resumed regular menses with estradiol 92 pg/mL and FSH 4.3 mIU/mL in the premenopausal range, and the discussion identifies this as "failure of goserelin ovarian ablation."

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 62-year-old man with abdominal distension, diffuse abdominal tenderness, and prominent superficial abdominal veins in the setting of advanced cirrhosis, and identify the cause of venous outflow obstruction.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient has a history of liver cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C, tobacco use, and post-stent coronary artery disease. He presented for elective left and right heart catheterization as part of a pre-transplant evaluation. He reports significant abdominal distension and pain. Therapeutic paracentesis did not improve the pain. He was found to have advanced cirrhosis, large esophageal varices on recent endoscopy, and thrombocytopenia, which made anticoagulation unsuitable.

Secondary_Symptoms: Pain not improved after therapeutic paracentesis

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Liver cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C, tobacco use, post-stent coronary artery disease.

Abdominal_Examination.Inspection: Abdominal distension with prominent superficial abdominal veins.

CT_Abdomen_With_Contrast.Findings: Occlusive thrombus of the inferior vena cava extending from the renal veins to the cavoatrial junction. Thrombus also observed in the portal vein. Multiple subcutaneous varicosities were present.

Inferior_Venogram.Findings: The inferior vena cava was 100% occluded 2 cm above the renal veins.

Right_Atrial_Contrast_Study.Findings: Occluded inferior vena cava at the junction of the right atrium.

Final_Venogram.Findings: Excellent flow in the inferior vena cava to the right atrium after stent placement and post-dilation.

Endovascular_Intervention.Findings: Right internal jugular and right femoral venous access were obtained. Balloon angioplasty alone did not restore flow. A 22 x 70 mm Wallstent was deployed in the inferior vena cava and post-dilated with an 18 x 60 Boston Scientific esophageal balloon. Small clots near the lower edge of the stent above the renal artery were removed with a 7-Fr snare.

Laboratory_And_Endoscopy.Anticoagulation_Contraindication_Context: Advanced liver failure with elevated INR, thrombocytopenia, and large esophageal varices on recent endoscopy.

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Inferior vena cava thrombosis with complete IVC occlusion, treated with endovascular stenting

title: Treatment of Inferior Vena Cava Thrombosis by Endovascular Stenting: A Case Report
diagnosis_evidence: Title states "Treatment of Inferior Vena Cava Thrombosis by Endovascular Stenting" and CT/venography showed an occlusive thrombus of the IVC extending from the renal veins to the cavoatrial junction with 100% occlusion; final venogram showed excellent flow after stent placement.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 45-year-old woman with recurrent, progressive ulcerated and nodular skin lesions of the right thigh, determine whether there is systemic involvement, and establish the most likely diagnosis.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient reports a long history of skin disease involving the right thigh, beginning in 2006 with an infiltrated nodule associated with ulcers that gradually enlarged. She has had prior surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy. In 2020, she noticed worsening skin lesions with several infiltrating nodules, scaling, crusted papules, and ulcerated plaques. One year after being lost to follow-up, she was referred again because of a major increase in skin lesions and new lesions on the thigh.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Progressive ulcerated and nodular skin lesions on the right thigh

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Type II diabetes treated with insulin. Primary hyperparathyroidism. Non-smoker.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: No fever, weight loss, or night sweats were reported. No pain, warmth, or mucosal involvement was reported. No visceral symptoms were reported in the source.

Skin_Examination.Findings: Ulcers and necrotic lesions, purplish, well-limited, of variable size, not painful, and not warm to the skin.

Skin_Biopsy_Histopathology.Microscopy: Cutaneous tissue with an infiltrate of large cells involving the dermis and showing epidermotropism. The infiltrate was composed of atypical cells with abundant pale eosinophilic cytoplasm, irregular elongated or lobed nuclei with prominent nuclear grooves and folds, fine chromatin, indistinct nucleoli, multinucleated giant tumor cells, high cytonuclear atypia, marked nuclear pleomorphism, and increased mitotic figures. Mixed inflammatory background with reactive lymphocytes, plasma cells, benign histiocytes, and eosinophils.
Skin_Biopsy_Histopathology.Interpretation: Findings consistent with Langerhans cell sarcoma.

Systemic_Assessment.Endocrine_Workup: Performed; cortisolemia and parathormone assessment were in favor of primary hyperparathyroidism

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Langerhans cell sarcoma of the skin (cutaneous Langerhans cell sarcoma)

title: Unusual Cutaneous Location of Langheransian Histiocytosis: A Case Report
diagnosis_evidence: The text explicitly states: "the diagnosis of Langerhans cell sarcoma was confirmed" after biopsy and immunohistochemistry showing CD1a and S100 positivity with high Ki-67.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 55-year-old man with sudden onset shortness of breath, three days of lumbar back pain, and vomiting, and identify the cause of severe acute kidney injury with metabolic/lactic acidosis and hyperkalemia.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient presents with sudden onset shortness of breath that woke him from sleep just prior to arrival. He reports three days of non-radiating lumbar back pain and two episodes of non-bloody emesis before the breathing difficulty began. He has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus and takes metformin, amlodipine, losartan, and atenolol.

Secondary_Symptoms: Three days of non-radiating lumbar back pain

General_Examination.Work_of_Breathing: Significant work of breathing with retractions and abdominal breathing

Respiratory_Examination.Auscultation: Lungs clear to auscultation with no wheezing, rhonchi, or rales

Abdominal_Examination.Findings: Mild epigastric tenderness with no rebound, guarding, or palpable pulsatile mass

Back_Examination.Findings: No costovertebral angle tenderness or midline tenderness elicited

Imaging.CT_Angiogram_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis: Obstructing stone at the left proximal ureteropelvic junction and at the right ureterovesical junction with resultant bilateral hydronephrosis

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Bilateral ureteral obstruction from obstructing ureteral stones causing acute kidney injury, severe metabolic/lactic acidosis, hyperkalemia, and metformin toxicity

title: Bilateral Ureteral Obstruction Causing Acute Kidney Injury and Resultant Metformin Toxicity
diagnosis_evidence: The title explicitly states "Bilateral Ureteral Obstruction Causing Acute Kidney Injury and Resultant Metformin Toxicity." CT showed an obstructing stone at the left proximal ureteropelvic junction and at the right ureterovesical junction with resultant bilateral hydronephrosis, and the patient had severe AKI with creatinine 14.7 mg/dL, anuria, profound acidosis, and elevated lactate that improved after bilateral nephrostomy, dialysis, and stenting.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 51-year-old woman with abdominal pain, altered mental status, hypothermia, hypoxia, and shock; identify the cause of severe metabolic/lactic acidosis and initiate urgent management in a primary care/acute assessment setting.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient has type 2 diabetes mellitus and recurrent urinary tract infections. She takes metformin 2000 mg/day. Two days before presentation she started feeling unwell and did not eat well. Immediately before admission, she became confused and had low blood glucose (24 mg/dL), prompting EMS treatment with oral glucose, intravenous dextrose, and intramuscular glucagon. On arrival she was confused and complained of severe epigastric abdominal pain.
Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, recurrent urinary tract infections, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 20-25%), chronic atrial fibrillation, pulmonary hypertension.

Vital_Signs.Blood_Pressure: 185/85 mmHg initially; later dropped to 57/33 mmHg in the ED

Abdominal_Examination.Findings: Tenderness to palpation in the epigastric region

Laboratory_Studies.Results: Laboratory studies were significantly deranged at presentation, including severe metabolic acidosis and lactic acidosis. Specific values are not specified in source.

CT_Abdomen.Findings: Bilateral patchy areas of pyelonephritis in the kidneys

Microbiology.Blood_Cultures: No organisms grew after five days

Clinical_Course_And_Response.Hemodynamics: Required norepinephrine and vasopressin after fluid resuscitation failed to improve hypotension
Clinical_Course_And_Response.Ventilation: Intubated for mechanical ventilation due to evolving septic shock and acute encephalopathy
Clinical_Course_And_Response.Acid_Base_Therapy: Received sodium bicarbonate boluses and bicarbonate with dextrose infusion for severe metabolic acidosis
Clinical_Course_And_Response.Renal_Replacement_Therapy: Emergent CRRT initiated with high bicarbonate dialysate; pH normalized and lactic acidosis resolved within 24 hours
Clinical_Course_And_Response.Outcome: CRRT discontinued after four days; extubated after 30 hours; vasopressors stopped within 72 hours; kidney function returned to baseline on day 5

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Metformin-associated lactic acidosis precipitated by pyelonephritis-induced septic shock

title: Metformin-associated Lactic Acidosis Successfully Treated with Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy
diagnosis_evidence: The case explicitly states, 'The diagnosis of concomitant metformin associated lactic acidosis was made, with pyelonephritis-induced septic shock being the inciting factor.' The title also identifies metformin-associated lactic acidosis.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess a 70-year-old woman presenting with diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, worsening consciousness, hypotension, and suspected severe metabolic derangement; identify the cause and initiate urgent management.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient has had 3 days of repeated diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting. She was reportedly normal until 3 days before admission, when these symptoms began. On the day of admission, her consciousness deteriorated. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage G3a, colon polyps, and depression. She has been taking metformin 1000 mg/day for more than 3 years without dose change.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting for 3 days
Secondary_Symptoms: Deteriorating consciousness on the day of admission
Weakness or severe illness appearance may be present

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage G3a, colon polyps, depression.
Patient_Actor.Medication_History: Metformin 1000 mg/day for more than 3 years without changing the dosage.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: No evidence of infection was found on whole-body computed tomography. Other review of systems findings are not specified in source.

Imaging.Whole_Body_CT: Maxillary sinusitis and a uterine fibroid; no evidence of infection.

Other.Response_To_Treatment: Lactate level and pH completely normalized within 72 hours after urgent CRRT.

OSCE_Examination.Correct_Diagnosis: Metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA) with acute kidney injury

title: A patient with metformin-associated lactic acidosis successfully treated with continuous renal replacement therapy: a case report
diagnosis_evidence: The title explicitly states "A patient with metformin-associated lactic acidosis successfully treated with continuous renal replacement therapy," and the case text shows severe lactic acidosis with markedly elevated metformin level in a patient taking metformin, consistent with MALA.
